meta.one_liner: Chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease driven by the IL-23 / IL-17 axis; a mature, biologics-led market now moving toward oral targeted therapy.
meta.coverage_note: Demonstration atlas, corroborated by a live public-API pull on 2026-07-23 — ClinicalTrials.gov (901 interventional psoriasis studies), Open Targets (946 associated targets, 167 drug candidates), and openFDA labels. The pipeline, standard-of-care and MoA structure match the live data; company attribution and prose are curated because raw trial sponsors are noisy (e.g. a shared trial can mis-tag a drug's sponsor). Epidemiology, market and sales figures are from the cited web sources. Efficacy percentages are approximate, cross-trial figures unless a head-to-head is cited.

headline_stats.label: Approved MoA classes
headline_stats.detail: TNF, IL-12/23, IL-17, IL-23, PDE4, TYK2, AhR, topicals

overview.definition: Plaque psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory disease that produces well-demarcated, scaly erythematous plaques, most commonly on the scalp, elbows, knees and trunk. It accounts for ~80–90% of psoriasis cases and is frequently accompanied by psoriatic arthritis and cardiometabolic comorbidity.
overview.pathophysiology: Aberrant activation of the IL-23 / IL-17 axis drives keratinocyte hyperproliferation and inflammation. Dendritic-cell–derived IL-23 sustains pathogenic Th17 cells, which release IL-17A/F and TNF, amplifying the inflammatory loop that maintains plaques.
overview.state_of_play: Psoriasis is one of the great success stories of targeted immunology: selective IL-23 and IL-17 biologics now deliver near-clear skin (PASI 90) in the majority of moderate-to-severe patients. The frontier has shifted from efficacy to (1) convenience and cost — oral targeted agents (TYK2, oral IL-23) challenging injectables — and (2) access, with the field still under-treated. TNF biosimilars and, from 2025, Stelara biosimilars are reshaping economics.

epidemiology.summary: Psoriasis affects roughly 125 million people worldwide (2–3% of the population) with marked geographic variation (higher in high-latitude, high-income regions). In the US it affects ~3.0% of adults (>7.5 million), rising with age and highest in White populations.

segments.name: Mild (topical-managed)
segments.note: limited body-surface area; managed with topicals ± phototherapy

epidemiology.demographics: Bimodal onset (peaks ~20s and ~50s); affects sexes roughly equally; highest prevalence in White populations (US 3.6%) vs Asian (2.5%) and Black (1.5%). ~30% of patients develop psoriatic arthritis; elevated cardiovascular, metabolic and depression risk.
epidemiology.unmet_need: Despite excellent biologics, a large share of moderate-to-severe patients remain untreated or under-treated (cost, access, injection aversion, specialist bottlenecks). Oral options with biologic-like efficacy, durable remission/disease modification, and treatment of hard-to-treat sites (scalp, nails, palmoplantar, genital) remain the key gaps.

patient_journey.description: Clinical diagnosis by dermatologist/PCP; often delayed; comorbidity screening (PsA, cardiometabolic) frequently missed.
Topical corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs, and non-steroidals (roflumilast, tapinarof); phototherapy for wider disease.
Moderate-to-severe patients escalate to oral systemics (apremilast, deucravacitinib, methotrexate) or biologics.
IL-23 / IL-17 biologics give durable near-clearance; switching driven by loss of response, access or comorbidity (e.g. concurrent PsA/IBD).

biology.summary: The IL-23 / IL-17 axis is the validated core of psoriasis pathogenesis. IL-23 (p19/p40) from dendritic cells maintains pathogenic Th17 cells; downstream IL-17A/F and TNF drive keratinocyte activation. Intracellular TYK2 transmits IL-23 (and type-I IFN) signalling — the basis of oral TYK2 inhibition. Open Targets ranks the top disease-associated targets as IL12B (0.74), TYK2 (0.71), IL23A (0.64), TNF (0.61), PDE4A (0.61), NR3C1/glucocorticoid receptor (0.60), VDR (0.60) and IL17A (0.60) — mirroring the drugged mechanism classes below.

pathways.name: IL-23 → Th17 → IL-17 axis
pathways.role: Central pathogenic loop; target of the most effective biologics.

pathways.name: TNF signalling
pathways.role: Amplifies inflammation; the first successful biologic target.

pathways.name: TYK2 / JAK-STAT
pathways.role: Intracellular transducer of IL-23 and type-I IFN; drugged orally (allosteric TYK2).

pathways.name: cAMP / PDE4
pathways.role: Immunomodulatory; PDE4 inhibition raises cAMP to dampen cytokine output (oral & topical).

targets.name: Interleukin-23 subunit p19
targets.rationale: Selective IL-23 blockade = best-in-class efficacy + durable, infrequent dosing.

targets.name: Interleukin-17A
targets.rationale: Rapid, high skin clearance; the effector cytokine.

targets.name: IL-12/23 subunit p40
targets.rationale: First IL-23-pathway target (ustekinumab); now bettered by selective p19.

targets.name: Tumor necrosis factor
targets.rationale: Legacy target; broad but less skin-selective; biosimilar era.

targets.name: Tyrosine kinase 2
targets.rationale: Enables oral targeting of the IL-23 pathway with biologic-adjacent efficacy.

targets.name: Phosphodiesterase 4B
targets.rationale: Oral and topical immunomodulation; modest efficacy, good convenience/safety.

subtypes.name: Plaque (vulgaris)
subtypes.description: ~80–90% of cases; the focus of this atlas.

subtypes.name: Guttate / inverse / erythrodermic
subtypes.description: Less common morphologies; different triggers and management.

subtypes.name: Pustular (GPP)
subtypes.description: Rare, severe; distinct IL-36 biology — adjacent indication (spesolimab).

subtypes.name: With psoriatic arthritis
subtypes.description: ~30% comorbidity; steers drug choice toward agents with joint efficacy.

standard_of_care.paradigm: Care is severity-driven. Mild disease (~80%) is managed topically (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs, and newer non-steroidals roflumilast and tapinarof) ± phototherapy. Moderate-to-severe disease steps up to oral systemics (deucravacitinib, apremilast, methotrexate) or biologics, where selective IL-23 and IL-17 agents are now preferred for their durable near-clearance. Drug choice is shaped by comorbidity — PsA favours IL-17/TNF/IL-23 with joint data; concurrent IBD favours IL-23 and avoids IL-17.

lines.note: IL-23/IL-17 preferred; choice tuned to comorbidity

top_products.note: WW sales incl. IBD; biosimilars from 2025

class_share.basis: approx. share of psoriasis biologic sales
approx. share of psoriasis biologic sales

guidelines.name: AAD–NPF psoriasis guidelines
guidelines.note: US treatment guidance (biologics, systemics, topicals, comorbidity screening).

moa_landscape.rationale: Selective IL-23 blockade is the current efficacy + durability benchmark in psoriasis.
moa_landscape.mechanism: Anti–IL-23 p19 mAbs starve pathogenic Th17 cells of their maintenance signal.

moa_landscape.rationale: Blocking the effector cytokine gives the fastest, deepest skin clearance.
moa_landscape.mechanism: Neutralise IL-17A (± IL-17F) or the IL-17 receptor.

moa_landscape.rationale: First oral agent to approach biologic-class efficacy; anchors the oral-targeted shift.
moa_landscape.mechanism: Allosteric inhibition of the TYK2 pseudokinase domain blocks IL-23/type-I IFN signalling.

moa_landscape.rationale: Pioneered the IL-23 pathway; now being displaced by selective p19 and facing biosimilars.
moa_landscape.mechanism: Anti-p40 blocks both IL-12 and IL-23.

moa_landscape.rationale: The original psoriasis biologic class; now legacy/value tier in the biosimilar era.
Holy grail: IL-23-pathway efficacy in a once-daily pill.
moa_landscape.mechanism: Oral peptide competitively blocks the IL-23 receptor.
cons: Phase 3; efficacy vs injectable IL-23 to be proven

moa_landscape.rationale: Convenience/safety tier; oral apremilast and topical roflumilast for milder disease.
moa_landscape.mechanism: Raise intracellular cAMP to dampen pro-inflammatory cytokines.

moa_landscape.rationale: Foundational first-line and steroid-sparing maintenance for mild disease and sensitive sites.
moa_landscape.mechanism: AhR agonism (tapinarof) and vitamin-D-receptor modulation normalise keratinocyte differentiation.

moa_landscape.rationale: Low-cost legacy systemics still widely used globally where biologics are inaccessible.

pipeline.summary: A crowded, mature clinical pipeline. The approved core is fully built out across IL-23, IL-17, TNF, IL-12/23, PDE4, TYK2 and topicals; near-term innovation is concentrated in ORAL targeted therapy (oral IL-23R peptide icotrokinra, next-gen oral TYK2 zasocitinib) and differentiated IL-17 (nanobody sonelokimab), all in Phase 3.

companies.name: AbbVie
companies.positioning: IL-23 leadership (Skyrizi) as its post-Humira immunology anchor; legacy TNF (Humira).

companies.name: Johnson & Johnson
companies.positioning: Broadest psoriasis franchise: p40 (Stelara), IL-23 (Tremfya) and the lead oral IL-23R peptide (icotrokinra).

companies.name: Novartis
companies.positioning: First-mover IL-17 (Cosentyx) with a large rheumatology footprint.

companies.name: Eli Lilly
companies.positioning: IL-17 (Taltz) plus IL-23 (mirikizumab) expanding from IBD into psoriasis.

companies.name: UCB
companies.positioning: Differentiated dual IL-17A/F (bimekizumab) with best-in-class complete-clearance rates.

companies.name: Bristol Myers Squibb
companies.positioning: Oral pathway pioneer with first-in-class TYK2 (Sotyktu).

companies.name: Takeda
companies.positioning: Next-gen oral TYK2 (zasocitinib) acquired via Nimbus, chasing Sotyktu.

companies.name: MoonLake Immunotherapeutics
companies.positioning: Single-asset IL-17A/F nanobody (sonelokimab) — a differentiated, acquirable clinical-stage story.

companies.name: Arcutis / Organon
companies.positioning: Non-steroidal topicals (roflumilast, tapinarof) modernising first-line care.

market.summary: A large, growing, biologics-led market. 2024 estimates cluster around $27–33B and forecasts reach ~$39–51B by the early 2030s (CAGR ~8–11%), though firms differ on scope and methodology. Value is concentrated in IL-23 and IL-17 franchises; growth drivers include the oral-targeted shift, expanded access, and switching — partly offset by TNF and (from 2025) Stelara biosimilar erosion.
drivers: Oral targeted therapy (TYK2, oral IL-23R) expanding the treatable pool
Continued penetration of selective IL-23/IL-17 in under-treated moderate-to-severe patients
Offset: TNF and Stelara biosimilar erosion
market.pricing_access: US list prices for biologics are high (tens of thousands/yr) with heavy rebating; access is gated by payer step-edits (often TNF-first) and prior authorization. Oral agents and biosimilars are reshaping cost dynamics; ex-US access varies widely and is a major driver of global under-treatment.

opportunity.title: Oral biologic-level efficacy
opportunity.rationale: An oral matching injectable IL-23 efficacy (icotrokinra, next-gen TYK2) could convert injection-averse and undertreated patients.

opportunity.title: Durable remission / disease modification
opportunity.rationale: No approved therapy is curative; treatment is chronic. Durable drug-free remission is the biggest unmet scientific goal.

opportunity.title: Hard-to-treat sites & special populations
opportunity.rationale: Persistent efficacy and QoL gaps at difficult sites and in underserved groups.

opportunity.title: Global access
opportunity.rationale: Biosimilars + low-cost orals to reach the large under-treated global population.

catalysts.significance: Accelerates share shift toward selective IL-23 and orals.
First oral targeting the best-validated pathway; could redefine the oral segment.
Next-gen oral TYK2 vs Sotyktu benchmark.
Differentiated IL-17A/F nanobody; key value inflection for a single-asset biotech.

evidence.summary: Cross-trial PASI 90 at ~week 16 anchors comparative efficacy (approximate; cross-trial unless a head-to-head is cited). Selective IL-23 and dual IL-17A/F lead on durable near-clearance; oral TYK2 sits below biologics but well above oral PDE4.

landmark_trials.name: ERASURE / FIXTURE
landmark_trials.result: ~77–82% response; FIXTURE showed superiority to etanercept

weaknesses: Extremely crowded, well-served biologic space — high bar to differentiate
opportunities: Hard-to-treat sites, pediatrics, and global access
threats: Need for head-to-head superiority to win share
